topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Albright smoothed the expensive, cream-colored envelope on his mahogany desk, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the wood. It was addressed simply, signed with a familiar, looping script: 'From Elias.' A slow, tight knot formed in his chest, a familiar, unpleasant clenching sensation that had been building since Elias had graduated and subsequently moved on without a backward glance. Albright stared at the stationery, the pristine white mocking the messy, lingering resentment he felt. He didn't need the contents; the gesture itself felt like a deliberate provocation, a challenge to the carefully constructed distance he had maintained since the disastrous final project grade. He picked up a letter opener, turning it over and over in his fingers, the metallic click echoing the sharp, unwelcome tension in his jaw.

[story 2]

"Well, look what the tide dragged in," Albright muttered, his voice a low, gravelly sound that barely disturbed the silence of his study. He didn't look up immediately, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon light slicing through the window. When he finally did, his eyes narrowed, assessing the gift—a rare, antique fountain pen, exquisitely crafted. A flicker of something sharp, almost acidic, shot through him. He remembered Elias, always so earnest, always so painfully aware of his own perceived shortcomings. Albright felt a hot flush creep up his neck, a reaction he furiously tried to suppress. He slid the letter across the desk, his movements stiff, every gesture calibrated to convey utter disdain. "I don't require... trinkets, Elias. Keep them."

[story 3]

The small, velvet box sat incongruously on the corner of Albright’s otherwise immaculate filing cabinet. He hadn't expected anything. The sheer audacity of the delivery—a discreet package left on his doorstep—made his stomach churn. He picked up the box, the weight of the contents feeling heavy and loaded. Inside was a collection of rare first-edition poetry, a subject he had publicly dismissed as frivolous years ago. A strange, almost painful tightening occurred in Albright’s chest. He ran a hand over his tired face, his expression hardening into a mask of cool indifference. He didn't open the box right away. Instead, he placed it deliberately on the floor, a silent declaration that whatever Elias intended to achieve with this display was entirely irrelevant to his current existence.
